friction: CM primitives exist, but support is implementation- and policy-specific
why_it_discourages_or_blocks_cm: A QUIC stack can expose migration primitives while browsers, application APIs, or default policies still suppress runtime migration.
repo_claim_scope: Implementation maturity is not binary; report API/test/observability maturity separately.
next_proof_needed: Runtime browser and mobile-client migration trials with policy artifacts.

friction: HTTP/3 application use must be proven before CM can be tested
why_it_discourages_or_blocks_cm: A browser cannot demonstrate HTTP/3 CM if the target application request never used HTTP/3; Alt-Svc and DNS ALPN hints are not enough.
repo_claim_scope: Local forced-H3 and selected public natural-H3 controls are usable as scoped H3 evidence; natural discovery remains target-specific.
next_proof_needed: Controlled public WebPKI origin baseline with server H3 request log and qlog.

friction: The trigger may not change the active client path
why_it_discourages_or_blocks_cm: Interface toggles or scripted commands can complete successfully while the route/interface/public IP used for the QUIC path does not change.
repo_claim_scope: Inactive-interface controls are negative controls, not CM evidence.
next_proof_needed: Before/after client path snapshot showing active route or public IP change.

friction: Tuple changes can be replacement sessions rather than CM
why_it_discourages_or_blocks_cm: Browser workloads may open another QUIC session even without a real network change, so server tuple change alone is insufficient.
literature_terms_any: Chromium QUIC NetLog migration event types;qlog
repo_claim_scope: Tuple change is only a supporting signal; require session continuity plus path validation.
next_proof_needed: NetLog/qlog/server evidence that target workload stayed on the migrated session.

friction: Load balancers need CID-aware routing across tuple changes
why_it_discourages_or_blocks_cm: Traditional 5-tuple routing can send migrated packets to the wrong backend; QUIC-aware routing needs routable CIDs or shared connection state.
repo_claim_scope: AWS NLB TCP_QUIC/QUIC positive and negative controls support a scoped LB claim.
next_proof_needed: Repeat with controlled public HTTP/3 browser workload through the same CID-aware path.

friction: HTTP/3 proxy support does not imply CM support
why_it_discourages_or_blocks_cm: A proxy may terminate QUIC, fail path validation, or create a new upstream connection, breaking end-to-end CM semantics.
repo_claim_scope: HAProxy negative control supports the claim that H3 support alone is not enough.
next_proof_needed: Add one positive QUIC-aware proxy or tunneling baseline if the paper needs proxy scope.

friction: CDN HTTP/3 CM can be edge-level rather than end-to-end
why_it_discourages_or_blocks_cm: Managed CDNs often terminate viewer QUIC at the edge; viewer-edge continuity should not be described as origin-end-to-end CM.
repo_claim_scope: Use CDN evidence only for edge-continuity framing unless origin QUIC is controlled.
next_proof_needed: Controlled edge test plus origin log separation if CDN scope is included.

why_it_discourages_or_blocks_cm: Connection migration changes IP/port while keeping QUIC state, stressing NAT, firewalls, rate limiters, Kubernetes conntrack, and operational monitoring.
literature_terms_any: QASM;RFC 9312;Secure Middlebox-Assisted QUIC;Connection Migration in the Wild
repo_claim_scope: Use as operational-reluctance explanation, not as a local performance result.
next_proof_needed: Optional Mininet or firewall reproduction only if the paper claims middlebox behavior directly.

friction: CM and preferred-address behavior can be operationally sensitive
why_it_discourages_or_blocks_cm: Migration can be repurposed for IP masking, censorship circumvention, exfiltration, or state-table abuse, making operators cautious.
repo_claim_scope: Use as related-work rationale for disabled or conservative deployment policies.
next_proof_needed: Do not overclaim unless testing a specific security appliance or policy.

friction: Downlink-dominant workloads may not trigger timely migration recovery
why_it_discourages_or_blocks_cm: If the client has no post-change data to send, path validation or failure detection can be delayed; application heartbeats or retries may recover the task while still hiding transport/session failure.
repo_claim_scope: Local downlink/heartbeat controls and upload retry controls justify comparing silent-client heartbeat and retry variants without treating task recovery as CM success.
next_proof_needed: Controlled public active path-change trials with and without heartbeat or retry.

friction: Browser and network artifacts are individually ambiguous
why_it_discourages_or_blocks_cm: NetLog hints, tuple changes, qlog frames, and route snapshots each miss part of the story; publishable CM evidence needs a combined chain.
literature_terms_any: qlog;Chromium QUIC NetLog;Connection Migration in the Wild
repo_claim_scope: The current rubric and classifiers enforce a combined evidence chain.
next_proof_needed: Complete final browser handover rows with all required artifacts.

friction: HTTP/3 adoption does not equal CM adoption
why_it_discourages_or_blocks_cm: Internet-wide studies find uneven CM support among HTTP/3-capable servers; provider configuration dominates observed support.
literature_terms_any: Connection Migration in the Wild;QUICstep;QUIC Hunter
repo_claim_scope: Use as the main gap that motivates controlled failure-layer experiments.
next_proof_needed: Run controlled public browser handover protocol and compare with wild-scan framing.

friction: Successful migration can still impose stall, retransmission, or QoE cost
why_it_discourages_or_blocks_cm: Even when migration works, handover latency and retransmission behavior can affect real-time media and task continuity.
repo_claim_scope: Current work verifies completion more than QoE; keep performance claims scoped.
next_proof_needed: Measure recovery time, stall duration, and user-visible task success in final browser trials.
